content:
---
name: git-workflow
description: Branch and merge workflow for small teams with trunk-based habits and clear ownership.
allowed-tools: bash git
---

## Branch naming

Create feature branches from `main` using `type/short-topic` (e.g. `feat/auth-cookie`, `fix/null-deref`). One logical change per branch; split unrelated work.

## Daily sync

Before coding, `git fetch origin` and `git rebase origin/main` (or merge if your team forbids rebase on shared branches). Push with `--force-with-lease` only on your own feature branch after a rebase.

## Merge and cleanup

Open a PR when tests pass locally. Squash-merge to `main` after approval. Delete the remote branch in the PR UI. If CI fails post-merge, revert the merge commit rather than fixing on `main` without a branch.

## Hotfixes

Branch from the tagged release for production-only patches; forward-port the same commit to `main` via cherry-pick or a follow-up PR so drift does not accumulate.

---
name: code-review
description: PR review checklist with quality gates before merge approval.
allowed-tools: bash git
---

## Before you start reviewing

Confirm the PR targets the correct base branch and has a clear title and description linking tickets or specs. Skim the diff size; if it is huge, ask the author to split before a deep review.

## Correctness and scope

Verify behavior matches the stated goal. Watch for off-by-one errors, wrong error codes, and missing null checks. Flag scope creep: unrelated refactors belong in separate PRs.

## Tests and observability

Require tests for new logic paths and regressions. Check that failures would be visible: logs at appropriate levels, metrics or traces where operations are critical.

## Security and data

Look for secrets in code or logs, unsafe deserialization, and authz gaps (user A accessing user B’s resources). Ensure PII is not logged.

## API and compatibility

For public APIs, confirm versioning or backward compatibility. Document breaking changes and migration steps in the PR body.

## Merge criteria

Approve only when CI is green, required reviewers have signed off, and open comments are resolved or explicitly deferred with a ticket. Use “request changes” when blockers remain; do not merge with silent TODOs on critical paths.

## Performance and cost

Flag N+1 queries, unbounded loops on user input, and accidental O(n²) sorts on large lists. Ask for benchmarks or complexity notes when hot paths change.

## Accessibility and i18n

For UI changes, verify focus order, labels, and screen-reader text. Ensure error strings are translatable and not built from concatenated fragments.

## Dependencies

Inspect lockfile diffs for transitive upgrades, license changes, and known-bad versions. Prefer pinning or overrides when a transitive dependency repeatedly breaks CI.

## When to block vs. nit

Block merges on security issues, data-loss risk, or red CI. Use comments for style nits; batch nits to avoid review ping-pong.

## Handoff and SLA

If you cannot finish within the team SLA, say so and reassign. Approve only lines you have actually reviewed; partial skim is worse than a second reviewer.

## Release hygiene

For user-visible behavior, confirm CHANGELOG or release notes are updated. Link migration docs for API or config changes.


When the PR touches auth or billing, require a second reviewer from that domain before merge.

---
name: typescript-strict
description: TypeScript strict mode migration patterns without freezing the codebase for weeks.
---

## Baseline the compiler

Enable `strict`, `strictNullChecks`, and `noImplicitAny` in `tsconfig.json` in a dedicated branch. Run `tsc --noEmit` and capture the error count; fix or suppress file-by-file only as a last resort.

## Fix order

Prefer fixing leaf modules (utilities, types) before consumers. Replace `any` with `unknown` and narrow at boundaries (parse, HTTP handlers). Use discriminated unions for variant results instead of optional piles of fields.

## Third-party gaps

If a library lacks types, add `types` in `package.json` or a minimal `*.d.ts` in `src/types/`. Prefer `@types/*` when available; avoid `as any` except behind a single adapter function with a comment.

## CI gate

Add a CI step that fails on `tsc` errors. If you must allow legacy files temporarily, use `// @ts-expect-error` with a ticket ID, not blanket `skipLibCheck` long term.

## Failure paths

If strict mode breaks runtime behavior you relied on (e.g. optional chaining hiding bugs), add explicit checks and tests rather than reverting compiler options.

## Editor and DX

Enable `strict` in the IDE and fix surfaced errors as you touch files. Use project references for large monorepos to keep incremental builds fast.

## Tests

Add tests that fail under loose types but pass under strict when behavior is clarified (for example undefined vs. empty string).


Record weekly `tsc` error counts in the team wiki so burn-down stays visible.

---
name: api-design
description: RESTful API design conventions, versioning, and error shapes for stable clients.
---

## Resources and verbs

Model nouns as resources (`/users`, `/users/{id}/orders`). Use POST for creates, GET for reads, PATCH for partial updates, DELETE for removal. Avoid verbs in URLs except for true RPC-style actions (`/actions/reset-password`) documented as non-REST.

## Status codes

Return `201` with a `Location` header on create. Use `204` for successful deletes with no body. Use `400` for client validation errors, `401`/`403` for auth failures, `404` when the resource identity is unknown, `409` for conflicts (duplicate, stale version).

## Versioning

Prefer URL prefix versioning (`/v1/...`) for public APIs. Deprecate with `Sunset` and `Deprecation` headers and a documented timeline. Never remove fields without a major version or compatibility layer.

## Pagination and filtering

Use cursor-based pagination for large sets; document default `limit` and max. Accept filter query params with a whitelist to avoid arbitrary SQL.

## Error body

Return a consistent JSON shape: `code`, `message`, optional `details[]` with field-level errors. Do not leak stack traces in production.

## Edge cases

Idempotency: support `Idempotency-Key` on POST for payments and irreversible side effects. Document rate limits and return `429` with `Retry-After`.

## Content negotiation

Support `Accept` and `Content-Type` for JSON vs. binary; return `406` when the client cannot be satisfied. Document charset defaults (`UTF-8`).

## Time and money

Use ISO-8601 UTC for timestamps; document timezone for calendar fields. Represent money in minor units (integer) with explicit currency code.

## Bulk and async

For large operations, return `202` with a job id and a poll URL; avoid multi-minute POST bodies. Document eventual consistency windows.

## Deprecation mechanics

Sunset headers must include a machine-readable date. Keep old behavior until the advertised date unless a critical security fix forces earlier removal.

## Client errors

Distinguish validation (`400` with field errors) from semantic errors (`422`). Never use `200` with an error payload for REST endpoints.

## Observability

Include a request id in responses and error bodies; propagate trace context headers through gateways.


Document charset, compression, and max body size on every public endpoint table in the spec.

---
name: docker-multi-stage
description: Docker multi-stage builds that keep production images small and reproducible.
allowed-tools: bash docker
---

## Stage layout

Use a `builder` stage with full toolchain (Node, gcc, git) and a final `runtime` stage with only the runtime (e.g. `node:20-alpine`). Copy artifacts between stages with `COPY --from=builder`.

## Dependency install

In the builder, copy lockfiles first and run install before copying source to maximize cache hits. Use `npm ci` or `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` in CI.

## Non-root user

Create a `USER` in the final stage with numeric UID/GID. Do not run as root in production; verify file permissions on copied assets.

## Health and signals

Use `exec` form for `CMD` so PID 1 receives signals. Add `HEALTHCHECK` for HTTP services or document external probes in Kubernetes.

## Failure paths

If the image is huge, inspect layers with `docker history`. If builds are slow, check cache invalidation from copying `.` too early. Scan images in CI with Trivy or similar.

## Build args

Pass `BUILDKIT_INLINE_CACHE` or registry cache to speed CI. Avoid secrets in build args; they persist in image history.

## Distroless and shells

If you need a shell only for debugging, use a debug image tag in non-prod; production image stays minimal.


Tag images with `git rev-parse HEAD` and build timestamp to trace regressions to commits.

---
name: ci-pipeline
description: CI pipeline stages from install through deploy with clear gates and artifacts.
allowed-tools: bash git
---

## Install

Check out with shallow clone if appropriate. Install dependencies with a frozen lockfile. Cache dependency store keyed by lockfile hash.

## Lint and typecheck

Run formatter check (fail if diff), linter, and `tsc --noEmit` or equivalent before tests. Fail fast on lint to save machine time.

## Test

Run unit tests in parallel where safe. Upload coverage; enforce a minimum on default branch or block regressions.

## Build

Produce versioned artifacts (container digest, tarball). Tag with `git sha` and semver when releasing. Store artifacts in the registry or artifact store, not only on the runner.

## Deploy

Deploy to staging automatically on `main`; production requires manual approval or tagged release. Roll back using previous artifact digest, not rebuild-from-old-commit unless reproducibility is broken.

## Failure handling

On red main, notify channel and block further merges until fixed or reverted. Keep deploy scripts idempotent; retry transient cloud errors with backoff.

## Secrets in CI

Inject secrets from the CI vault; never echo them in logs. Mask known patterns in output; rotate if leaked.

## Matrix and forks

Run a minimal matrix on PRs from forks without secrets; full matrix on trusted branches. Document which jobs are skipped and why.

## Artifact retention

Set TTL on build artifacts and test reports; large binaries belong in object storage with lifecycle rules.

## Performance budget

Fail PRs that increase bundle size or cold-start time beyond thresholds when those metrics are tracked.

## Deploy verification

After deploy, run smoke checks (health endpoint, canary metrics). Auto-rollback on SLO burn or error-rate spikes when safe.

## Compliance hooks

Run license and SBOM generation on release tags; attach artifacts to the release for audit trails.


Block deploy jobs unless the artifact digest passed the prior stage’s checksum verification.

---
name: kubernetes-pods
description: Kubernetes pod specs with probes, resources, and sensible defaults.
allowed-tools: bash kubectl
---

## Requests and limits

Set `requests` to the steady-state need and `limits` to cap bursts; omitting limits allows noisy neighbors. For Java or Node, align heap flags with memory limits.

## Probes

Use `startupProbe` for slow boots, `livenessProbe` only if restart fixes the state, `readinessProbe` for traffic membership. Avoid the same endpoint for liveness and readiness if readiness includes dependencies.

## Security context

Run as non-root, read-only root filesystem where possible, and drop capabilities. Mount secrets as volumes, not env vars, when feasible.

## Edge cases

OOMKilled: raise limit or fix leak; do not only raise without profiling. CrashLoopBackOff: check logs and events; fix probe timing before disabling probes blindly.

---
name: terraform-modules
description: Terraform module boundaries, inputs, and remote state practices.
---

## Module shape

One module per deployable concern (VPC, database, app service). Expose inputs for names, tags, and environment; avoid hard-coded account IDs in shared modules.

## State

Use remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB lock, Terraform Cloud, etc.). Separate state per environment or per blast-radius unit. Never edit state manually except during documented recovery.

## Composition

Root module wires child modules and passes outputs. Keep `terraform.tfvars` per env out of git if secrets; use CI variables or vault.

## Lifecycle

Use `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` on irreplaceable data stores after validation. Document `terraform import` steps when adopting existing resources.

## Failure paths

If `plan` shows unexpected destroys, stop and investigate dependency changes. Use `moved` blocks or `state mv` for renames, not destroy-and-recreate in prod without a window.

## Providers

Pin provider versions in `required_providers`; upgrade in a dedicated PR with full plan review.

## Outputs and contracts

Document output shapes; use `sensitive` for secrets. Add contract tests or `terraform validate` in CI for every module.


Run `terraform fmt -check` in CI and fail if formatting drifts from canonical style.

---
name: unit-testing
description: Unit test layout, isolation, and assertions that catch real regressions.
allowed-tools: bash vitest
---

## Arrange-act-assert

Structure tests so setup is obvious: build inputs, call one unit, assert outputs or thrown errors. One behavioral expectation per test name.

## Isolation

Mock external I/O at boundaries (HTTP, DB, clock). Use fake timers for debounce and retries. Avoid testing implementation details like private method call order unless behavior demands it.

## Data builders

Use small factories for valid objects; vary one field per test for edge cases (empty string, max length, unicode).

## Failure paths

Assert rejection messages or error codes, not only `toThrow()`. If tests flake, fix async handling (`await`/`waitFor`) before increasing timeouts.

---
name: integration-testing
description: Integration tests for HTTP APIs using real processes and controlled data.
allowed-tools: bash docker vitest
---

## Test environment

Spin up API and dependencies via docker-compose or testcontainers. Use a dedicated test database; run migrations before the suite and truncate or reset between tests that need isolation.

## HTTP contract

Call real routes with `fetch` or supertest. Assert status, JSON shape, and headers (`Set-Cookie`, `Location`). Cover 4xx validation and 5xx paths where applicable.

## Seeding

Seed minimal fixtures per scenario. Avoid relying on production-like volume; instead assert ordering with explicit data.

## Parallelism

If tests run in parallel, use unique tenant IDs or serial execution for shared DB tests. Tag slow tests for optional CI jobs.

## Failure diagnosis

On failure, print response body and server logs from the test harness. Quarantine flaky tests: fix or delete; do not retry-hide indefinitely.

## Time and randomness

Freeze clocks or inject a time provider in tests that depend on "now." Seed random generators for reproducible failures.

## Auth in tests

Mint short-lived JWTs or session cookies via the same code paths as production; do not bypass auth middleware unless testing public routes.


Fail the suite if unclosed DB connections or handles remain after each file.

---
name: e2e-playwright
description: End-to-end browser tests with Playwright, stable selectors, and CI reliability.
allowed-tools: bash npx playwright
---

## Project setup

Initialize with `npx playwright install` in CI. Pin browser versions. Use `test.describe` per feature and `test.step` for readable reports.

## Selectors

Prefer `data-testid` or user-visible roles over CSS chains that break on redesign. Avoid `waitForTimeout` except as last resort; use `expect(locator).toBeVisible()` and auto-waiting.

## Auth

Use storage state or API login to skip UI login in every test. Rotate test credentials; never use prod accounts.

## Parallel and sharding

Shard across workers in CI with `--shard`. Isolate tests that mutate shared server state.

## Flakes

Retry only in CI with a low count; fix root cause (race, animation, network). Capture trace and video on first retry for debugging.

## API mocking

Use `page.route` for third-party outages in tests, but keep a smoke test against a sandbox if contract matters.

## Failure paths

If CI is green locally but red remotely, compare timezone, env vars, and base URL. Verify deterministic date handling in UI.

## Environment parity

Match base URL, feature flags, and clock to CI. Seed data via API where faster than UI; keep seeds idempotent.

## Network conditions

Occasionally run with throttling profiles to catch race assumptions; do not make it the default because it slows CI.

## Visual regression

Use screenshot tests sparingly for critical layouts; tolerate OS font rendering differences with thresholds or a dedicated Docker image.

## Accessibility checks

Run axe or Playwright accessibility assertions on critical flows; fail on serious violations unless tracked with waiver.

## Debugging CI

On failure, upload trace zip and har as CI artifacts. Re-run locally with `DEBUG=pw:api` when reproducing flakes.

## Test data cleanup

Delete created users or tenants in `afterAll` when tests create resources; handle partial failures so the next run does not collide.

---
name: jwt-auth
description: JWT access and refresh token flow with rotation and secure storage guidance.
---

## Token roles

Use short-lived access JWTs (minutes) and long-lived refresh tokens (days) stored server-side or in httpOnly cookies. Never put secrets in JWT claims.

## Signing and algorithms

Use asymmetric keys (RS256) for verification across services; rotate keys with `kid` in header. Reject `none` and unexpected algorithms.

## Validation

Verify `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, and `nbf` on every request. Clock skew: allow small leeway server-side only after understanding risk.

## Refresh rotation

Issue a new refresh token on each use; invalidate old refresh on reuse (reuse detection). Rate-limit refresh endpoint.

## Logout and compromise

Maintain a blocklist or version user sessions in DB for instant revoke. Document incident steps: rotate signing keys and invalidate all refresh tokens.

## Edge cases

Mobile: prefer secure storage over AsyncStorage for refresh material. CSRF: use SameSite cookies and double-submit or separate token for cookie-based APIs.

## Storage transport

Prefer httpOnly Secure cookies for refresh tokens in browsers; pair with CSRF defenses for cookie-based APIs.

## Service-to-service

Use mTLS or signed service tokens in addition to JWTs when crossing trust boundaries inside the cluster.

---
name: secrets-management
description: Secrets rotation, vault usage, and leak response for applications and CI.
---

## Classification

Separate bootstrap secrets (CI OIDC, vault token) from app secrets (DB URLs, API keys). Never commit secrets; use git-secrets or trufflehog in pre-commit and CI.

## Storage

Store runtime secrets in Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or equivalent with IAM-scoped access. Inject at runtime as env or files; avoid printing in logs.

## Rotation

Define rotation period per sensitivity. Support dual-read during rotation: accept old and new until cutover, then revoke old. Automate rotation APIs where the provider allows.

## CI/CD

Use OIDC to cloud roles instead of long-lived cloud keys in CI. Scope secrets to branch and environment.

## Incident

If leaked, revoke immediately, rotate all dependent credentials, and audit access logs. Post-incident: add detection and reduce secret lifetime.

## Edge cases

Local dev: use `.env.local` gitignored and a sample `.env.example` without values. Document how to obtain dev secrets through SSO or one-time bootstrap.

## Auditing

Enable audit logs on secret read paths; alert on anomalous volume or new callers.

## Break-glass

Document emergency access that bypasses normal rotation; time-bound and reviewed after use.

---
name: dependency-audit
description: Automated dependency scanning and triage for supply-chain risk.
allowed-tools: bash npm pnpm
---

## Tooling

Run `npm audit` or `pnpm audit` in CI weekly and on lockfile changes. Add OSV-Scanner or Snyk for broader coverage and transitive C deps if applicable.

## Policy

Treat critical/high as merge blockers unless waived with ticket and expiry date. Medium/low: schedule fixes in sprint hygiene.

## Upgrades

Prefer minor/patch bumps in grouped PRs. For breaking majors, read changelogs and run full test suite.

## False positives

Document accepted risks in `audit-ci` config or equivalent with justification and review date.

---
name: postgres-indexing
description: PostgreSQL indexing for read-heavy workloads with EXPLAIN-driven tuning.
allowed-tools: bash psql
---

## Measure first

Capture slow queries from `pg_stat_statements` and log lines over `duration`. Run `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)` on representative parameters, not only ideal ones.

## B-tree basics

Create indexes that match filter and join columns left-to-prefix. For `WHERE a = ? AND b > ?`, index `(a, b)` often works; verify with EXPLAIN.

## Covering indexes

For frequent read-only lookups, consider `INCLUDE` columns to avoid heap fetches when the planner can use an index-only scan after vacuum.

## Partial indexes

Index `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` or `WHERE status = 'active'` when queries always constrain the same subset; smaller index, faster writes.

## Write cost

Every index slows inserts/updates/deletes. Drop unused indexes found via `pg_stat_user_indexes` and `idx_scan = 0` after monitoring window.

## Edge cases

Low cardinality columns alone may not help; consider composite or partial. `ILIKE '%foo%'` cannot use normal B-tree; use `pg_trgm` or full-text search.

## Failure paths

If the planner ignores your index, check statistics (`ANALYZE`), row estimates, and parameter sniffing; use `SET plan_cache_mode` only for diagnosis, not permanent hacks.

## Multicolumn order

Put equality columns before range columns in composite indexes. Re-run EXPLAIN when data distribution shifts seasonally.

## Expression indexes

Index expressions used in WHERE (`lower(email)`) only if queries cannot be rewritten to use a functional constraint or citext.

## Maintenance

Schedule `VACUUM` and `ANALYZE` after bulk loads; `REINDEX CONCURRENTLY` when bloat is confirmed by `pgstattuple` or similar.

## Partitioning

For very large tables, partition by time or tenant; index each partition and attach constraints that match partition keys.

## Read replicas

Queries on replicas may see lag; do not assume read-your-writes unless using routing or session stickiness with known lag bounds.

## Locking during index build

Prefer `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` in production; monitor for invalid indexes after failures and rebuild.

## Connection pooling

Tune pool size vs. Postgres `max_connections`; watch for prepared statement bloat with PgBouncer in transaction mode.

---
name: redis-caching
description: Redis caching with TTLs, namespacing, and invalidation that stays consistent.
---

## Key design

Prefix keys with service and version (`app:v2:user:{id}`) to allow safe schema changes. Use TTL for soft freshness; document max staleness in API docs.

## Cache-aside

On read miss, load from DB, set Redis, return. On write, update DB then delete or update cache key; avoid updating only cache.

## Invalidation

Prefer explicit key deletes on mutations affecting that entity. For fan-out, maintain reverse indexes or version tokens in the cached blob.

## Stampede

Use single-flight or short lock to prevent thundering herd on hot keys. Add jitter to TTL to avoid synchronized expiry.

## Failure paths

If Redis is down, degrade to DB with circuit breaker; do not hang requests. Monitor memory eviction policy; avoid `noeviction` unless you understand OOM risk.

## Serialization

Use a versioned envelope for cached JSON; reject unknown versions and repopulate from DB.

## Cluster mode

Use hash tags in keys only when necessary; watch for hot slots and resharding during upgrades.


Alert when hit ratio drops below SLO for ten minutes after a deploy or config change.

---
name: database-migrations
description: Safe schema migrations with expand-contract phases and rollback planning.
allowed-tools: bash psql
---

## Expand-contract

Add new columns nullable or with defaults compatible with old code first. Deploy app that writes both old and new. Backfill in batches. Switch reads. Remove old path.

## Lock awareness

Avoid long table rewrites during peak; use concurrent index creation in Postgres. For large backfills, chunk updates and sleep between batches.

## Rollback

Prefer forward fixes (new migration) over `down` that drops data. Test `down` only on disposable clones.

## Failure paths

If migration fails mid-deploy, have runbook: restore replica, or fix forward with idempotent migration. Never assume manual SQL in prod without peer review.

---
name: prompt-engineering
description: Structured prompts for reliable LLM outputs with evaluation hooks.
---

## Role and context

State who the model is, audience, and output format (JSON schema, markdown sections). Put stable instructions first; put variable user content under clear delimiters.

## Constraints

Ask for citations to provided documents only, or “say I don’t know” when missing. Set length bounds and enumerate allowed values for enums.

## Examples

Include 1–3 few-shot pairs matching the exact format you want. Negative examples help reduce common mistakes.

## Evaluation

Maintain a golden set of inputs with expected structure checks (not only exact string match). Re-run on model upgrades; track regression rate.

## Failure paths

If output drifts, tighten schema validation and add automatic repair pass (second call to fix JSON). If latency spikes, reduce max tokens and parallelize only where independent.

## Tool use

When models call tools, validate tool arguments with the same schema as your API; reject impossible combinations before execution.

## Cost controls

Set max tokens per step; summarize long contexts instead of blind truncation that drops constraints.


Log prompt version ids with production traffic to correlate quality changes with deployments.

---
name: rag-pipeline
description: RAG pipeline from chunking and embeddings to retrieval and grounded answers.
---

## Ingestion

Split documents on semantic boundaries (headings, paragraphs) with overlap. Store metadata: source, version, ACL.

## Embeddings

Pick embedding model and dimension; version the model id in the vector store metadata. Batch embed for throughput; handle rate limits with backoff.

## Retrieval

Hybrid search (BM25 + vector) often beats either alone for keyword-heavy queries. Retrieve top-k then rerank with a cross-encoder if budget allows.

## Grounding

Pass retrieved chunks into the prompt with citations. Ask the model to quote spans; validate that cited ids exist in the context pack.

## Failure paths

Empty retrieval: return “no sources” instead of hallucinating. Poisoned doc: enforce ACL at retrieval time, not only at UI.

## Ops

Monitor embedding drift when updating corpora; reindex on model change. Track latency p95 for retrieve + generate.

## Chunk quality

Drop boilerplate chunks; deduplicate near-identical sections. Cap chunk size so the model context fits multiple citations.

## Hygiene filters

Strip PII at ingest when policy requires; block secrets and private keys from embedding.

## Re-ranking costs

Cache embeddings and retrieval results for identical queries short-term; invalidate on document version bumps.

## Evaluation set

Maintain labeled questions with expected source ids; measure recall@k and citation accuracy, not BLEU.

## Drift

When the corpus or embedding model changes, compare retrieval overlap on the golden set before full rollout.

## Abuse

Rate-limit embedding and query endpoints; cap context size per tenant to prevent cost spikes.

## Multi-modal

If using images, store extracted text alongside embeddings and link back to page anchors for citations.

---
name: mcp-server
description: MCP server scaffolding, tools, and auth boundaries for LLM clients.
allowed-tools: bash node npm
---

## Responsibilities

Expose tools with JSON schemas; validate inputs server-side. Do not trust model-generated parameters without checks.

## Transport

Support stdio for local dev and HTTP/SSE for remote with TLS. Document session lifecycle and cancellation.

## Authz

Map client identity to allowed tools and data scopes. Rate-limit expensive tools.

## Errors

Return structured errors the client can surface; never leak stack traces to untrusted callers.

---
name: documentation
description: Technical docs that lead with outcomes and embed runnable examples.
---

## Audience

Write for the next maintainer on call: prerequisites, what breaks if misconfigured, and where logs live. Put the happy path first, then failure modes.

## Structure

Start with “What you get” and “When to use this.” Then installation, configuration table, and one copy-paste example that works on a fresh machine.

## Examples

Every CLI flag or env var mentioned should appear in an example. Mark deprecated options with removal timeline.

## Diagrams

Use a single sequence or component diagram for flows with more than three hops; keep it updated in the same PR as code changes.

## Maintenance

Add a “Last verified” date for external dependencies. In reviews, reject docs-only PRs that contradict the code.

## Edge cases

Document idempotency, retries, and known platform differences (Linux vs macOS paths).

## Troubleshooting index

Maintain a table of symptoms, checks, and fixes. Link to runbooks for outages and dashboards for SLOs.

## Search and discovery

Use consistent page titles and headings so site search and AI assistants surface the right doc.

---
name: incident-response
description: Incident workflow from detection through stabilization and blameless post-mortem.
---

## Roles

Assign Incident Commander, Communications, and Scribe. IC owns decisions; others execute. Rotate if fatigue sets in.

## Stabilize

First reduce blast radius: feature flags off, scale up, block bad traffic, failover if rehearsed. Prefer known rollbacks over novel fixes under pressure.

## Communicate

Send initial status within minutes: impact, workaround if any, next update time. Update on meaningful change, not every five minutes of silence.

## Evidence

Timestamp decisions in the war room doc. Preserve logs and configs before they rotate; snapshot metrics dashboards.

## Customer and data

If data exposed, loop legal/compliance before public statements. Notify customers per policy and regulator timelines.

## Post-incident

Within 48 hours, run a blameless review: timeline, root cause, contributing factors, what went well, action items with owners. Track actions to completion; verify in staging.

## Failure paths

If rollback fails, have a rehearsed break-glass procedure. If multiple incidents overlap, declare priority and pause non-critical releases.

## Severity and priority

Classify by customer impact and data exposure, not noise volume. Escalate when error budget burns faster than rollback can absorb.

## Coordination tools

Use a single incident channel; pin the status doc. Avoid parallel decision threads that contradict the IC.

## Third parties

If a vendor is root cause, open their status page ticket while mitigating locally. Document vendor comms timestamps.

## Degraded mode

When full recovery is slow, ship read-only or feature-reduced mode with clear user messaging and support macros.

## Regulatory and PR

Coordinate external comms with legal; never speculate on breach scope in public channels.

## Action item tracking

Convert post-mortem items into tickets with owners and due dates; review completion in the next sprint retrospective.

## Game days

Schedule failure injection exercises on non-prod quarterly; update runbooks when procedures fail in practice.
